title: XOR and Triangle
description: This time, the pink soldiers have given you an integer $$$x$$$ ($$$x \ge 2$$$). Please determine if there exists a positive integer $$$y$$$ that satisfies the following conditions. $$$y$$$ is strictly less than $$$x$$$. There exists a non-degenerate triangle $$$^{\text{∗}}$$$ with side lengths $$$x$$$, $$$y$$$, $$$x \oplus y$$$. Here, $$$\oplus$$$ denotes the bitwise XOR operation . Additionally, if there exists such an integer $$$y$$$, output any. $$$^{\text{∗}}$$$A triangle with side lengths $$$a$$$, $$$b$$$, $$$c$$$ is non-degenerate when $$$a+b  \gt  c$$$, $$$a+c  \gt  b$$$, $$$b+c  \gt  a$$$.
input_spec: Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 2000$$$). The description of the test cases follows. The only line of each test case contains a single integer $$$x$$$ ($$$2 \le x \le 10^9$$$).
output_spec: For each test case, print one integer on a separate line. The integer you must output is as follows: If there exists an integer $$$y$$$ satisfying the conditions, output the value of $$$y$$$ ($$$1 \le y  \lt  x$$$); Otherwise, output $$$-1$$$. If there exist multiple integers that satisfy the conditions, you may output any.

routes.name: XOR-AND identity simplifies triangle inequality
routes.description: Use the identity x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y) to reduce the three triangle inequalities to two conditions on bits: y must share at least one set bit with x, and y must have at least one set bit that x does not. This characterizes exactly when a valid y exists and directly yields a construction.

observations.title: Rewrite the triangle sides using the XOR-AND identity
observations.description: For any non-negative integers x and y, the identity x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y) holds. This lets you express the third side x XOR y as x + y - 2*(x AND y). Substituting this into the three triangle inequalities transforms them from geometric constraints into algebraic conditions on the bitwise AND of x and y.

hints.nudge: How are addition and XOR related for binary numbers? What role does the carry play?
hints.question: If you write x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y), can you express x XOR y purely in terms of x, y, and x AND y? How does this simplify x + y > x XOR y?
hints.formal: Since x + y = (x XOR y) + 2*(x AND y), the third side is x XOR y = x + y - 2*(x AND y). Substituting into x + y > x XOR y gives x + y > x + y - 2*(x AND y), i.e., x AND y > 0.

observations.title: Two of the three inequalities are automatic when y < x
observations.description: After substitution, the inequality x + y > x XOR y becomes x AND y > 0 (they share a set bit). The inequality x + (x XOR y) > y simplifies to x > x AND y, which is always true when y < x because x AND y <= y < x. So only two conditions actually matter: x AND y > 0, and the third inequality.

hints.nudge: Substitute c = x + y - 2*(x AND y) into all three triangle inequalities. Which ones are trivially satisfied given y < x?
hints.question: The second inequality x + c > y expands to 2x - 2*(x AND y) > 0. Since x AND y <= y < x, is this always positive?
hints.formal: Inequality 2: x + (x XOR y) > y becomes 2x - 2*(x AND y) > 0, i.e., x > x AND y. Since x AND y <= y < x, this always holds. Inequality 1: x + y > x XOR y becomes x AND y > 0.

observations.title: The binding constraint: y must not be a submask of x
observations.description: The third inequality y + (x XOR y) > x simplifies to y > x AND y, meaning y must have at least one bit set that is NOT set in x. Equivalently, y must not be a submask of x. If y were a submask of x, then x AND y = y, and the triangle degenerates (the sum equals x, not strictly greater).

hints.nudge: What does the third inequality y + (x XOR y) > x reduce to after substitution? What does it say about the bits of y?
hints.question: If every set bit of y is also set in x (y is a submask of x), what is x AND y? What happens to the inequality y > x AND y?
hints.formal: y + (x XOR y) > x becomes 2y - 2*(x AND y) > 0, i.e., y > x AND y. This fails exactly when y = x AND y, i.e., y is a submask of x. So y must have at least one bit set outside the bit-pattern of x.

observations.title: Characterize when no valid y exists
observations.description: A valid y must satisfy three things: y < x, x AND y > 0 (shared bit), and y is not a submask of x (extra bit). No such y exists in exactly two cases. If x is a power of 2 (single bit k), every y < x has bits only below k, sharing no bit with x. If x = 2^k - 1 (all bits 0 through k-1 set), every y < x is a submask of x, so no y has an extra bit outside x.

hints.nudge: For which x does every y in [1, x-1] fail one of the two conditions? Think about what the binary representation of x looks like.
hints.question: If x = 2^k, what bits can y < x have? Can any of them overlap with x's single bit? If x = 2^k - 1, is every smaller number a submask?
hints.formal: If x = 2^k: only bit k is set. All y < 2^k have bits in {0,...,k-1}, so x AND y = 0 always. If x = 2^k - 1: bits {0,...,k-1} are all set, and every y < x is a subset of these bits, so y is always a submask. In all other cases, x has at least one 0-bit and at least one 1-bit below the MSB, allowing a valid y.

observations.title: Construct y by choosing one shared bit and one extra bit
observations.description: When a valid y exists, you can build it with just two bits: pick any bit position that is set in x (to satisfy x AND y > 0), and pick any bit position that is NOT set in x but is below x's most significant bit (to satisfy y not a submask of x, and y < x). Setting y to the OR of these two single-bit values gives a valid answer.

hints.nudge: You need y to share a bit with x and also have a bit that x lacks. What is the simplest y with exactly those properties?
hints.question: If bit i is set in x and bit j is not set in x (with j less than the MSB of x), what is y = (1 << i) | (1 << j)? Does it satisfy all conditions?
hints.formal: Let i be any position where x has a 1-bit, and j be any position where x has a 0-bit with j < MSB(x). Set y = (1 << i) | (1 << j). Then x AND y >= (1 << i) > 0, and y has bit j not in x, so y is not a submask. Since both bits are below MSB(x), y < x. This runs in O(log x) per test case.

routes.name: Small brute force over two-bit candidates
routes.description: Observe that if a valid y exists, one with at most two bits set also works. Enumerate all y = (1 << i) | (1 << j) for bit positions i, j in [0, 29] and check the triangle inequality directly. At most 900 candidates per test case.

observations.title: A valid y needs exactly two bit-level properties
observations.description: From the triangle inequality analysis, y must share at least one set bit with x (so x AND y > 0) and must have at least one set bit not in x (so y is not a submask). The simplest y satisfying both properties has exactly two bits: one shared with x and one not in x.

hints.nudge: What is the minimum number of set bits y needs to simultaneously share a bit with x and have a bit outside x?
hints.question: Can a y with a single set bit satisfy both x AND y > 0 and y not a submask of x at the same time? What about two set bits?
hints.formal: y needs bit i in x (for AND > 0) and bit j not in x (for non-submask). If i != j, y needs at least 2 bits. If i = j, the conditions contradict. So the minimum is two bits (or one bit if i = j is somehow possible, which it is not).

observations.title: x < 2^30, so only 30 bit positions matter
observations.description: Since x <= 10^9 < 2^30, both x and any valid y have at most 30 bits. This means the search space for two-bit candidates y = (1 << i) | (1 << j) with i, j in [0, 29] is at most 30 * 30 = 900 values, which is tiny.

hints.nudge: How many bit positions are relevant given the constraint x <= 10^9?
hints.question: If you enumerate all pairs of bit positions (i, j) where 0 <= i, j <= 29, how many candidate y values is that?
hints.formal: 10^9 < 2^30, so all bits of x and y fit in positions 0 through 29. The number of two-bit candidates (1 << i) | (1 << j) for i, j in {0,...,29} is at most 30^2 = 900. When i = j, y = 1 << i has a single bit.

observations.title: Check each candidate directly against y < x and the triangle inequality
observations.description: For each candidate y = (1 << i) | (1 << j), verify: y < x, and the three triangle inequalities for sides x, y, x XOR y. If any candidate passes all checks, output it. If none pass, output -1. No algebraic simplification of the inequalities is needed for this approach.

hints.nudge: You have at most 900 candidates. What do you need to check for each one?
hints.question: Given a candidate y, what are the three conditions for x, y, x XOR y to form a non-degenerate triangle? Can you just check them directly?
hints.formal: For each y in {(1 << i) | (1 << j) : 0 <= i, j <= 29}: if y < x and x + y > (x ^ y) and x + (x ^ y) > y and y + (x ^ y) > x, output y and stop. If no candidate works, output -1. Total work: O(900) per test case.
